request.question: Who's album which reflects on his depressive state, had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976?

title: Come to Me (France Joli song)
Love Machine (The Miracles song)
Bardeux
Lean into It
Extra Texture (Read All About It)
Jeff Dawson
Right Back Where We Started From (album)
Manu Gavassi (album)
Attitudes (band)
Welcome Back (John Sebastian album)
sentences: "Come to Me" is a #1 disco hit from 1979 performed by France Joli, who had recorded it at the age of fifteen with producer, Tony Green, who composed the song and briefly sings on it.
The track also features the famed Philadelphia session vocalists, The Sweethearts of Sigma Sound.
The song was introduced on the album "France Joli", which was released in the US on 17 April 1979 on Prelude, and rose to #26.
On the Billboard Hot 100.
"Come to Me" received a major boost on 7 July when Joli performed it as a last minute replacement for Donna Summer at a concert held on Fire Island, whose estimated audience numbered 5000.
"Come to Me" began a three-week reign atop the Billboard Hot Dance Club Play on 22 September 1979.

"Come to Me" peaked at #15 on the Hot 100 and at #36 on the R&B chart, a decent showing for a new artist, but also a symptom of the increasing disenchantment of Top 40 radio stations with disco music.
"Love Machine" is a 1975 single recorded by Motown group The Miracles, taken from their album "City of Angels".
This song was a #1 Pop smash on the "Billboard Hot 100", and the biggest-selling hit single of The Miracles' career.
This single was one of two Billboard Hot 100 Top 20 hits recorded by The Miracles with Billy Griffin as lead vocalist; the other is 1973's "Do It Baby".
Griffin had replaced Miracles founder Smokey Robinson as lead singer in 1972.
The song features a growling vocal by Miracle Bobby Rogers, with group baritone Ronnie White repeating ""Yeah Baby"" throughout the song.
Bardeux is a dance duo from the 1980s.
The group was formed in 1986 and consisted of Stacy "Acacia" Smith and Tairrie B.
The group's first single "Three Time Lover", went to #10 on Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart.
Tairrie B left the group to pursue a solo career and was replaced by Lisa "Jaz" Teaney.
In 1988 the group released their album "Bold as Love" and their second single "Magic Carpet Ride".
The single went to #5 on Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart and #81 on the "Billboard" Hot 100.
The group performed the song on Club MTV.
"When We Kiss", the third single from the album, would turn out to be the group's biggest hit going all the way to #36 on the Billboard Hot 100.
The fourth single released from the album, "Bleeding Heart", was a top 40 hit on the Billboard's Hot Dance Singles Sales chart.
A fifth and last single release from that album, "Hold Me, Hold Me" did not chart.
Lean into It is the second studio album by the American rock supergroup Mr. Big, released in 1991.

The band's breakthrough release, "Lean into It" peaked at number 15 on the "Billboard" 200 charts, while the single "To Be with You" became the band's first and only song to hit number 1 on the Billboard Hot 100.
The follow-up single, "Just Take My Heart", was another Top 40 hit, peaking at number 16 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Extra Texture (Read All About It) is the sixth studio album by English musician George Harrison, released in September 1975.
It was Harrison's final album under his contract with Apple Records and EMI, and the last studio album issued by Apple.
The release came nine months after his troubled 1974 North American tour with Ravi Shankar and the poorly received "Dark Horse" album.
The melancholic mood of the recording reflects Harrison's depressed state at the criticism generated by these projects.
Jeff Dawson is a Canadian record producer, song writer, engineer, and mixer.
Vancouver-based Jeff Dawson developed and produced the breakthrough self-titled second album for Daniel Powter.
After working on Daniel's songs and helping develop the project's sound over 3 years, Warner Bros. Chairman/CEO Tom Whalley took one listen and knew he had something extraordinary.
Daniel's single "Bad Day" was #1 for 5 weeks on Billboard Hot 100 of 2006 and has become an incredible world wide hit with almost 2.2 million downloads and nearly 3 million albums sold.
Dawson produced State of Shock's hit single "Money Honey" (certified platinum in Canada) cementing Top 10 status at multiple Canadian radio formats, including Rock, CHR, and Hot AC.

Along with becoming the #1 most played/requested song at Satellite radio selling 40,000 copies prior to its US release, surpassing platinum status for digital sales with over 51,000 sold, "Money Honey" also held down the #1 post on the Canadian all formats Radio Chart for two months, spending close to a year on the Canadian Billboard Hot 100 singles Chart.

Right Back Where We Started From is the debut album by British R&B and soul music singer Maxine Nightingale The title track was first released as a single in the autumn of 1975 and reached #8 in the UK .
In the US it reached #5 in Adult Contemporary, #9 in Dance Music/Club Play, #46 in R&B singles and #2 in the Billboard Hot 100.
It was the success of this single to which prompted her to return to London to complete a Right Back Where We Started From album.
The album was released in the US early 1976 to enthusiastic reaction, reaching #65 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Another single from the album, “Gotta Be the One”, charted at #53 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Manu Gavassi is the debut album of Brazilian singer-songwriter Manu Gavassi.
The album contains thirteen tracks, the sound is defined with strong influence on teen pop and pop rock.
The first single from the album was "Garoto Errado".
The song was released in June 11, 2011 and peaked at number 22 in Brazil's Billboard Hot 100.
The second single from the album was "Planos Impossíveis" and was released in January 25, 2011.
The song received positive reviews from critics and peaked at number 26 in Brazil's Billboard Hot 100.
The album was produced by Rick Bonadio and released through Universal Music Brazil's subsidiary, Midas Music, on October 12, 2010.
Attitudes was a rock band from Los Angeles consisting of keyboardist David Foster, guitarist Danny Kortchmar, bass guitarist Paul Stallworth and drummer Jim Keltner.
Its four members came together while working as session musicians during the recording of George Harrison's album "Extra Texture (Read All About It)".
One single by the band, "Sweet Summer Music" reached #94 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 in 1976.
James Taylor later covered their single "Honey Don't Leave L.A." and that version reached #61 on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1978.
Welcome Back is an album by American singer/songwriter John Sebastian, released in 1976 (see 1976 in music).
It reached number 79 on The Billboard 200 chart.
Two singles were released from the album with the title song "Welcome Back" reaching number one on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and Adult Contemporary charts.
It also reached number 93 on the Country Singles chart.
The second single, "Hideaway" peaked at number 95 on the Billboard Hot 100.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

response.hop1_query: Who's album which reflects on his depressive state, had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976?
hop1_docs: Klique | Klique was an American R&B trio, consisting of Howard Huntsberry, Isaac Suthers and his sister, Deborah Suthers.  They released four albums, starting with "It's Winning Time" in 1981, concluding with "Love Cycles" in 1985.  Their only song to make the US "Billboard" Hot 100 chart was a cover version of Jackie Wilson's 1960 hit, "Stop Doggin' Me Around," which reached #50 on the Hot 100 and #2 on the US "Billboard" R&B chart in 1983.  In total, they had nine songs on "Billboard"' s R&B chart.

Break It to Me Gently (Aretha Franklin song) | "Break It to Me Gently" is a song written by Carole Bayer Sager and Marvin Hamlisch which was an R&B hit for Aretha Franklin in 1977.  Released from the "Sweet Passion" album, it reached Number 1 on the Hot Soul Singles chart in June 1977.  The song's success was only faintly reflected on the "Billboard" Hot 100 with a Number 85 peak, before dropping out of the Hot 100 after two weeks.  "Break It to Me Gently" would be Franklin's final Atlantic single to appear on the Hot 100 - from which she would be absent until 1980.

Jibbs | Jovan Campbell (born November 13, 1990), better known by his stage name Jibbs, is an American rapper.  He had one top ten single on the "Billboard" Hot 100, which was his debut single "Chain Hang Low".  Jibbs' only other song that charted on the "Billboard" Hot 100 is "King Kong" (featuring Chamillionaire).  Jibbs is currently working on his next studio album, "Ten Years Later", to be released in 2017.

Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song) | "Show Me the Way" is a song written by Peter Frampton, which was originally released on his 1975 album "Frampton" and as a single, but gained its highest popularity as a song from his 1976 live album "Frampton Comes Alive! ".  The song reached number 6 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and number 10 on the UK Singles Chart, becoming his biggest hit until "I'm in You" in 1977.

Tyga discography | The discography of Tyga, an American rapper, consists of four studio albums, two compilation albums, fourteen mixtapes, eight singles (including four as a featured artist) and forty-eight music videos.  In 2008, Tyga released his first studio album, "No Introduction", on the record label Decaydance Records.  The album featured the single "Coconut Juice", which features singer Travie McCoy; the song peaked at number 94 on the US "Billboard" Hot 100, becoming Tyga's first song to appear on the chart.  In 2010, Tyga and American singer Chris Brown released the collaborative mixtape "Fan of a Fan", which included the single "Deuces": the song peaked at number 14 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and became Tyga's first song to chart on the US Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs chart, peaking at number thirty.  Tyga also appeared on the song "Loyalty", a single by fellow rapper Birdman, and collaborated with rapper Lil Wayne on the non-album single "I'm on It", both of which failed to chart on the "Billboard" Hot 100.

Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright) | "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)" is a song by Rod Stewart, recorded at Muscle Shoals Sound Studio in Sheffield, Alabama for his 1976 album "A Night on the Town".  The song became his second US chart topper on the "Billboard" Hot 100.  spending 8 weeks at #1 from November 13, 1976 - January 1, 1977, peaked at No. 5 in the UK, No. 3 in Australia and charted well in other parts of the world as well.  It was the number 1 song on "Billboard"' s 1977 year-end chart.  It became the best-selling single of 1977 in the United States.

Lil Wayne singles discography | American rapper Lil Wayne has released 200 singles – including 44 as a lead artist – and twelve promotional singles. Lil Wayne attained his first singles chart entry in 1999 as a featured artist on Hot Boys member Juvenile's single "Back That Azz Up", which peaked at number 19 on the United States "Billboard" Hot 100 and became a top ten hit on the Hot R&B/Hip-Hop Songs and Hot Rap Songs charts.  Wayne later released his debut solo studio album "Tha Block Is Hot" in November 1999.  Its title track and lead single, which features B.G. and Juvenile, reached number 65 on the "Billboard" Hot 100. " Lights Out" followed in December 2000 and produced the singles "Get Off the Corner", "Everything" and "Shine". " Way of Life", the lead single from Wayne's third studio album "500 Degreez", peaked at number 71 on the Hot 100 and became a top 20 hit on the Hot Rap Songs chart.  In 2004, Wayne was featured on the single "Soldier" by American girl group Destiny's Child, which became his first top ten hit on the Hot 100 and enjoyed commercial success internationally.
hop1_titles: Break It to Me Gently (Aretha Franklin song)
Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song)
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Artists/albums with 1976 Billboard Hot 100 songs (Peter Frampton; Rod Stewart).

Facts supported by passages:
- Peter Frampton: song "Show Me the Way" was "originally released on his 1975 album 'Frampton'" and "gained its highest popularity as a song from his 1976 live album 'Frampton Comes Alive!'" and it "reached number 6 on the 'Billboard' Hot 100."
- Rod Stewart: song "Tonight's the Night (Gonna Be Alright)" was recorded for his 1976 album "A Night on the Town" and "became his second US chart topper on the 'Billboard' Hot 100," "spending 8 weeks at #1 from November 13, 1976 - January 1, 1977."

Ambiguities / missing facts:
- Missing fact: which specific "album which reflects on his depressive state" — neither passages mention any album reflecting on a depressive state. Bridge entity needed: the album title (and its artist) that is described as reflecting on the artist's depressive state.
- Candidate artists with 1976 Hot 100 songs in these passages: "Peter Frampton" (Frampton Comes Alive!) and "Rod Stewart" (A Night on the Town). The target album/artist is ambiguous among these candidates.

Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
1. Fetch "Frampton Comes Alive!" (article lede and Background/Reception/Track listing) to check whether that album is described as reflecting on Frampton's depressive state and to confirm the 1976 Hot 100 song sourcing.
2. Fetch "A Night on the Town (Rod Stewart album)" (lede/Background/Composition/Tracks) to check for any note that the album "reflects on his depressive state" and confirm the 1976 Hot 100 song sourcing.
3. Fetch artist pages "Peter Frampton" (Career/Personal life sections) and "Rod Stewart" (Career/Personal life) for mentions of albums reflecting on depressive state.

hop2_docs: Frampton Comes Alive! II | Frampton Comes Alive!  II is a 1995 live album by English musician Peter Frampton.  It is a sequel to his 1976 multiplatinum album "Frampton Comes Alive".  The title is styled as Frampton Comes Alive II on packaging.

Frampton Comes Alive! | Frampton Comes Alive!  is a double live album by English rock musician Peter Frampton released in 1976.  It is one of the best-selling live albums in the United States, and considered by many to be one of the finest live rock recordings of all time.  Following four solo albums with little commercial success, "Frampton Comes Alive!"  was a breakthrough for the artist.

Frampton (album) | Frampton is the fourth studio album by Peter Frampton, released in 1975 before he went on tour and recorded "Frampton Comes Alive! " The most recognizable songs from the album are "Show Me the Way" and "Baby, I Love Your Way" which became big hits when released as singles from "Frampton Comes Alive!"  The album peaked at #32 on the "Billboard" 200.

Hiatt Comes Alive at Budokan? | Hiatt Comes Alive at Budokan?  was singer-songwriter John Hiatt's twelfth album and first live album, released in 1994.  The album was actually recorded at venues in North America; the title parodies both Peter Frampton's "Frampton Comes Alive! " and the "At Budokan" albums released by numerous artists.

Hampton Comes Alive | Hampton Comes Alive is a six-disc live album by the American rock band Phish, released on November 23, 1999, by Elektra Records.  It is the band's third live album and the first time complete live Phish concerts were released in their entirety (though fan recordings of most Phish shows are widely circulated).  "Hampton Comes Alive" consists of two full concerts recorded on November 20 and 21, 1998, at the Hampton Coliseum in Hampton, Virginia.  The album title is a play on Peter Frampton's classic live album "Frampton Comes Alive! ".

Live in San Francisco, March 24, 1975 | Peter Frampton, Live In San Francisco, March 24, 1975 is a live recording by Peter Frampton and the precursor to the Record Breaking "Frampton Comes Alive."  It's a much more intimate show, with a smaller audience, broadcast on San Francisco's KSAN from Sausalito's Record Plant, literally only months before he recorded the Live album, "Frampton Comes Alive," that would break records as one of the best-selling live albums in the United States.

Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song) | "Show Me the Way" is a song written by Peter Frampton, which was originally released on his 1975 album "Frampton" and as a single, but gained its highest popularity as a song from his 1976 live album "Frampton Comes Alive! ".  The song reached number 6 on the "Billboard" Hot 100 and number 10 on the UK Singles Chart, becoming his biggest hit until "I'm in You" in 1977.
hop2_titles: Show Me the Way (Peter Frampton song)
response.summary_2: Peter Frampton had a song on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976. Peter Frampton's live album Frampton Comes Alive! featured "Show Me the Way," which reached number 6 on the Billboard Hot 100 (the provided passages do not state that any of his albums "reflect on his depressive state").
